agent_raw:
This retrospective evaluation has two critical problems that prevent it from justifying the proposed deployment. The first is label validity: the historical record of whether interpreters were joined and whether supervisors flagged communication problems reflects what dispatchers did under heterogeneous practices, not whether interpretation was genuinely needed. Some shifts routinely joined interpreters early; others relied on bilingual colleagues. The model trained on this data learns to predict dispatcher behavior, not communication need. When deployed to delay interpreter connection, the model would be changing the very policy its labels were generated under.

The second problem is the absence of stratified analysis. Emergency calls have dramatically different stakes: delaying interpretation on a suspected cardiac arrest has different consequences than delaying it on a parking dispute. The evaluation reports only aggregate precision and recall, so it is impossible to assess whether the model is safe for high-acuity incidents. The vendor's claim of 'minimal impact on high-acuity incidents' is asserted without evidence.

The override rule helps at the margins but does not fix either problem. The agency needs stratified analysis by call type and acuity, and ideally a prospective pilot that directly measures communication outcomes under the delay policy, before this retrospective evidence can justify deployment.
all_issues_raised: Labels reflect dispatcher behavior under old policy, not true interpretation need
No stratified metrics by call type or acuity
Override rule does not substitute for valid prospective evidence
